coredata.dc:title: Custom-tailoring tight nanocomposite membranes for advanced reclamation of hairwork dyeing effluent
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Yuefei
Bai, Xueshuang
Zhang, Nan
Lu, Zhaoyu
Zhu, Mengyao
Wang, Yongxin
Jiang, Kai
coredata.dc:description: A novel combination of custom-tailoring tight nanocomposite membrane process with dual-stage pretreatment incorporating flocculation and ultrafiltration has been experimentally conceived for the advanced reclamation of seriously contaminated hairwork dyeing effluent (HDE). The hybrid system exhibited impressive advantages of qualified recycle rate of HDE (91.2%) and minimal environmental impact when taking both bearable membrane fouling and appropriate long-term separation performance into consideration. It was observed that after partial alternative doping of M-tolidine-H and continuous incorporating of carbon quantum dots, both skin features (smoothness, hydrophilicity and charge) and nanoporous loose body (porosity and apparent pore size) of the resultant membranes were improved synergistically. As a consequence, the optimal TFN-CQDs membrane showed high water permeance of 4.31 L m−2 h−1 bar−1, rejection for small-sized organics (≥97.3%) and salt (≥96.4%) and good antifouling ability (86.2% of flux recovery ratio) operated for 72 h, which were superior than those of most other reported permeate membranes. Besides, the economical and feasible pretreatment steps including less dosage (only 100 mg L−1) of biodegradable CLF-10 flocculant and cheap UF module made the optimized reuse process more attractive. Overall, this study has taken a concrete step forward on the road of realizing zero liquid discharge and advanced reclamation of HDE.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanofiltration
Hairwork dyeing effluent
Membrane modification
Wastewater reclamation
Structure-activity relationship

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of CQDS and tight NF membranes
Characteristics and flocculation performance of CLF-10
Physicochemical properties of the resultant TFC/TFN membranes skin layers
Nominal pore size, PSD and porosity
Performance of TFN-CQDs membrane for advanced reclamation of HDE
Reusing performance comparison with other related studies

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Henan provincial key science and technology research project
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Henan Provincial Science and Technology Research Project

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by Henan provincial key science and technology research project (No. 192102210045 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of fabrication process for MTH/CQDs incorporated tight nanocomposite membranes.

Physicochemical properties as well as flocculation performance of CLF-10: (a–b) chemical composition, charge and wettability, (c–d) AFM scanning and height distribution, (e–f) particle size distribution and floc optical image.
ATR-FTIR (a) and XPS wide scan (b) spectra of the resultant TFC-0, TFC-MTH, and TFN-CQDs membranes.
Top surface, cross-sectional FESEM and surface AFM 3D images of TFC-0 (a, d, g), TFC-MTH (b, e, h) and TFN-CQDs (c, f, i) membranes.
Water contact angle (a) and zeta potential (b) measurements of the as-prepared TFC-0, TFC-MTH and TFN-CQDs membranes.
Pore size distribution and porosity of the resultant TFC-0, TFC-MTH and TFN-CQDs membranes skin-layers.

Performance comparison of the optimal TFN-CQDs membrane with its counterpart for advanced reclamation of HDE: (a) operating and fouling resistance data, (b) contaminant rejection and (c) permeate quality.
Contact angle (a), ATR-FTIR (b, c), CLSM scanning images (d, e) as well as fluorescence intensity profiles (f) of the virgin/fouled TFN-CQDs membrane and its counterpart.
The properties of raw HDE and product water after each treatment under the optimum operating conditions.
Comparative performance of TFN-CQDs membrane with other permeate membranes for series of textile effluents reuse.

head.ce:title: Custom-tailoring tight nanocomposite membranes for advanced reclamation of hairwork dyeing effluent

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Yellow River and Huai River Water Environmental and Pollution Control, Ministry of Education, Henan Key Laboratory for Environmental Pollution Control, School of Environment, Henan Normal University, 46 East of Construction Road, Xinxiang, 453007, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Yellow River and Huai River Water Environmental and Pollution Control
Henan Key Laboratory for Environmental Pollution Control

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Yellow River and Huai River Water Environmental and Pollution Control, Ministry of Education, Henan Key Laboratory for Environmental Pollution Control, School of Environment, Henan Normal University, 46 East of Construction Road, Xinxiang, 453007, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel combination of custom-tailoring tight nanocomposite membrane process with dual-stage pretreatment incorporating flocculation and ultrafiltration has been experimentally conceived for the advanced reclamation of seriously contaminated hairwork dyeing effluent (HDE). The hybrid system exhibited impressive advantages of qualified recycle rate of HDE (91.2%) and minimal environmental impact when taking both bearable membrane fouling and appropriate long-term separation performance into consideration. It was observed that after partial alternative doping of M-tolidine-H and continuous incorporating of carbon quantum dots, both skin features (smoothness, hydrophilicity and charge) and nanoporous loose body (porosity and apparent pore size) of the resultant membranes were improved synergistically. As a consequence, the optimal TFN-CQDs membrane showed high water permeance of 4.31 L m−2 h−1 bar−1, rejection for small-sized organics (≥97.3%) and salt (≥96.4%) and good antifouling ability (86.2% of flux recovery ratio) operated for 72 h, which were superior than those of most other reported permeate membranes. Besides, the economical and feasible pretreatment steps including less dosage (only 100 mg L−1) of biodegradable CLF-10 flocculant and cheap UF module made the optimized reuse process more attractive. Overall, this study has taken a concrete step forward on the road of realizing zero liquid discharge and advanced reclamation of HDE.

ce:section.ce:para: Xuchang city, situated on the central China's Henan province, produces more than 35% of the total global supply of hair products, and has built a characteristic industrial cluster with annual output value of 4 billion dollars [1]. As a typical high water consuming and heavily polluting industry, statistics show that the freshwater consumption, ammonia nitrogen (NH4 +-N), COD and wastewater production per ton of wig product are up to 225 t, 72 kg, 270 kg and 180 t, respectively [2]. From the point of view of existing local mainstream manufacturing technique, this large amount of hairwork effluent mainly comes from processes such as pickling, neutralization, dyeing and rinsing. Thereinto, dyeing and its attached rinsing processes have attracted more attention because the broadly defined hairwork dyeing effluent (HDE) generated by them accounts for 30.7% of the total amount of wig wastewater. The most contaminated and complex HDE is featured with high negative charge (zeta potential of −26.3 mV) and content of chromaticity (3200 times), turbidity (160 NTU), salinity (2.47‰), and several toxic azo colorants, as well as low biodegradability (BOD5/COD ratio between 0.187 and 0.206). This high-intensity point source pollution, even after the conventional standard discharge treatment (such as micro-electrolysis, coagulation/flocculation, hydrolytic acidification, anaerobic treatment and biological contact oxidation, etc) [1,3,4], still inevitably results in the gradual degradation of surface/ground water ecosystem near the industrial park. For instance, Du et al. fabricated a novel starch-based flocculant (St-g-PDMC-DQ) with branch chain architecture used for hairwork wastewater treatment, and only led to a better turbidity removal of 97.7% but an unsatisfactory UV254 removal of 60.1% under the optimal operating conditions of 20 mg L−1 at pH 3.0 [1]. Thus, based on the principle of “less input - more reuse - less discharge”, it is very important to carry out research on water saving and emission reduction technologies in hairwork dyeing process in order to improve the utilization rates of raw materials.

Reports in the literature manifested that due to the poor biodegradation of this kind of wastewater, physicochemical techniques, such as oxidation, adsorption, coagulation - flocculation (CF) and membrane filtration, and especially combination of them have shown unparalleled economic and technological advantages and environmental benefits in the advanced treatment or reuse of dyeing effluents in textile, printing and paper industries, etc [5,6]. Thereinto, due to only involving the outstanding separation not destruction of target contaminants, and their low capital cost and simple operation, a combined process of CF and integrated membrane system behaved all the more remarkable [7,8]. In our recent work, a shaggy and tree-shaped cationic cellulose flocculant (CLF-10) was prepared according to the inherent characteristics of HDE, and yielded impressive primary pretreatment performance compared to the classical CF system (polyaluminum chloride and polydiallyldimethyl ammonium chloride) [2,9]. Specifically, the corresponding turbidity, chromaticity and DOC of the supernatant were optimized from 16 NTU, 300 and 121 mg L−1 to 0.62 NTU, 100 and 54 mg L−1, respectively, at the expense of less than a tenth of the optimal dosage. Consequently, the overall product water recovery (R ov) of dual membrane process (ultrafiltration (UF), reverse osmosis (RO)) pretreated by flocculation increased safely from 76% to 80.3% while improving quality of recycled water and mitigating membrane fouling. However, the complexity and variability of HDE composition make it difficult to manage stable fluxes and control RO fouling, especially considering the need for a sustained and steady increase in HDE reuse rate [10,11].

Theoretically, it is also an important method to further improve the reuse rate of HDE by constructing novel anti-fouling membranes based on specific contaminant removal targets achieving a salt rejection >90%, a hardness rejection >95%, and nearly 100% removal of DOC [2,12]. Inspired by the high-level consistency between reclaimed water quality indexes and separation characteristics of more energy-efficient tight nanofiltration (NF) membrane (molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) < 400 Da) [13], it is of great significance to fabricate a new-type tight NF membrane with enhanced perm-selectivity and antifouling property as an alternative to RO, and carry out research on its structure-activity relationship for advanced reclamation of HDE after predetermined two-stage pretreatment. State-of-the-art tight NF membranes mainly involve thin-film composite (TFC) or thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) structure and consist of polyamide skin layer obtained by the interfacial polymerization (IP) reaction between m-phenylenediamine (MPD) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC) [14]. Researchers usually fine tune the skin layer formed by fully aromatic monomers with high degree of cross-linking to tight NF scope by incorporating semi-/nonaromatic reactive monomer with low amino/chloride group density allowing chain extension or even twisting [11,15], dispersing nanoparticles (NPs) such as mesoporous nanosilica, porous zeolite and non-porous carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in the aqueous phase [16,17], anchoring multicharged nanofilms on NF substrates [18], etc. Boo et al. found that the average pore radius of the resultant NF membrane incorporated with bipiperidine (BP) as a nonaromatic and long-chain amino monomer increased obviously from 0.47 nm to 0.61 nm when the BP content increased from zero to 0.5 wt% [11]. Yang et al. also verified that by increasing the polyethyleneimine molecular weight from 0.18 kDa to 70 kDa, the pore size of the prepared nanocomposite membrane gradually enlarged [15]. Liu and coworkers even argued that only dispersing porous NPs in the MPD aqueous phase or not organic TMC phase could substantially decreased the degree of cross-linking polymerization, and thus lead to form a polyamide layer with larger pore size [16]. In fact, these modified means, such as doped negatively charged CQDs as an emerging class of carbon-based NPs, are better known for significantly improving membrane permeability, anti-fouling resistance [19–21] and removing performance for the same-charged HDE solution system. Hence, we may expect finely tunable pore size and other associated skin features and body microstructure of the separation layer by systematically regulating the above strategies.

The aim of the current study is to fabricate novel tight, negatively charged TFN membranes with tailored rejection rates of DOC, hardness and salt in the process of advanced treatment of real HDE, through partial alternative doping of spiro-structured M-tolidine-H (MTH) allowing chain extension and continuous incorporating of CQDs into the selective layers. Also, improved separating and anti-fouling performance of the fabricated membranes under the possible maximum product water recovery was evaluated and compared to commercial NF/RO membranes. A series of physicochemical properties of the fabricated TFN membranes selective layers were extensively characterized beforehand, in order to clarify their structure-activity relationship. We further validated a stable long-term operation of the optimal nanocomposite NF membrane, highlighting the potential application of tight NF membrane with tailored pore size and other necessary microstructural features of functional layer for advanced reclamation of real HDE against a background of continuously minimizing waste (brine) stream.

Raw HDE was sampled from Longzheng hairwork manufacturing company located in Xuchang city and stored at 4 °C, and its main physicochemical characteristics are displayed the first column in Table 1 . The synthetic reagents for preparing CLF-10 flocculant including α-cellulose powder, Girard's reagent T, ethylene glycol, sodium periodate, fluorescein isothiocyanate, NaCl and HCl were all purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China), and the synthesized route was detailed in our previous work [2]. Self-made polyethersulfone (PES) based hollow fiber UF membrane module with MWCO of 50 kDa and an active area of 0.02 m2 was employed and used for producing secondary pretreatment effluent.

Flat sheet PES membrane purchased from RisingSun Membrane Company (Beijing, China) with MWCO of 50 kDa was used as NF substrate. M-phenylenediamine (MPD), trimesoyl chloride (TMC) and citric acid were supplied by Aladdin (Shanghai, China). MTH was obtained from Acme Chemical Co., Ltd (Zhengzhou, China). Sodium dodecyl sulphate (SDS) and n-hexane were supplied by Damao Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). BW30 membrane provided by Dow was adopted for performance comparison with the fabricated TFC/TFN membranes. All the chemicals mentioned above were AR-grade and used without further purification. Deionized (DI) water (2.0 μS cm−1) was used throughout this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of CQDS and tight NF membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The process of obtaining CQDS were performed according to a straightforward pyrolytic method [22]. Briefly, 4.0 g citric acid was put into a crucible and heated at 200 °C for 35 min to reach the molten state with orange appearance. After cooling down to ambient temperature and then adjusting its pH to 7.0 by the dropwise addition of 0.1 M NaOH solution under vigorous magnetic stirring condition, the aqueous CQDs solution could be obtained by using dialysis bag with a MWCO of 1000 Da in DI water for 48 h. Finally, solid CQDs (see the physicochemical properties in Fig. S1) was obtained by freeze-drying of the solution.

A series of CQDs/MPD/MTH-TMC TFN membranes were prepared by IP method on top of the PES substrate, as illustrated in Fig. 1 . For gaining tight NF membranes, firstly, MPD and MTH with different mass ratios but a fixed amount of primary amine groups and CQDs with different concentrations were dissolved in 0.1 wt% SDS aqueous solution by ultrasonication for 1 h to form homogeneous mixture. After that, the PES support membranes were tapped to glass plates and immersed into the aqueous solution for 10 min, followed by the removal of residual aqueous solution via draining vertically. Subsequently, a 0.05 w/v% organic solution of TMC/n-hexane was poured onto these monomer-saturated substrate membranes for 1 min to form polyamide functional layers. Finally, excess organic phase was drained and the resultant membranes were air-dried at 60 °C for 10 min for further polymerization reaction, rinsed thoroughly, and stored in DI water at 4 °C. Here, 0.42 wt% and 60 mg L−1 are considered to be the optimum loading concentration of MTH and CQDs for fabricating TFC/TFN membranes in this study on the basis of the perm-selectivity data of those resultant membranes containing both dopant concentrations from 0 to 0.48 wt% and 80 mg L−1, respectively (see Table S1). As a control, a nascent TFC membrane was also prepared via the same procedure by only employing 1.5 wt% MPD solution as aqueous phase. Hereafter, based on the expanded categories of doping agents or not in the aqueous phase, the resulting membranes were briefly labelled as TFC-0, TFC-MTH and TFN-CQDs, respectively.

Bench-scale advanced reclamation of real HDE experimental procedure in this research covers two parts: (1) primary flocculation of CLF-10 in series with secondary UF filtration on raw HDE; (2) the prepared tight NF and comparable RO permeation experiments fed with effluent after two-stage pretreatment.

Pretreatment process inherits our previous optimized steps [2]. In brief, after adding 100 mg L−1 CLF-10 flocculant, 20.0 L of raw HDE was stirred at 220 rpm for 60 s, 30 rpm for 480 s and then settled for 1 h in jar test. Afterwards, almost all the supernatant was extracted and subsequently drawn into UF module under predefined pressure and tangential-flow velocity of 1.0 bar and 2.15 × 10−2 m s−1, respectively, yielding the optimum UF filtrate recovery (R UF) of 98% accordingly.

10 L UF filtrate for each batch with satisfied quality (turbidity and total hardness in terms of CaCO3 values less than 0.01 NTU and 1000 mg L−1, respectively) was further pumped into NF/RO module to obtain qualified reclaimed water. Under the same operating parameters including initial transmembrane pressure of 8 bar, cross-flow velocity of 0.29 m s−1 and feedwater temperature of 25 °C, the pre-compacted NF/RO permeation tests were run on a semi-closed-loop operation and continued until the permeate recovery (R NF or R RO) approaching 96.0%. During this period, the above three parameters, as well as additional operating time and permeate mass, were recorded at 5-min intervals. NF/RO membrane separating performance for HDE advanced reuse was evaluated in terms of contaminant rejection (R, %) and permeate flux (J, L·m−2 h−1). For clarification, J, R and R M (M stands for three different membrane modules (UF, NF and RO)) were defined in Eqs. (1)–(3), respectively.

where V is the filtrate/permeate volume, t is the operating time and A is the membrane area; c f,i and c p,i are the concentrations of the rejected constituent i (TDS and DOC) in the feedwater and in the filtrate/permeate, respectively. Q is the flow rate; subscripts p, f and r refer to the permeate, feed and recycle streams, respectively. Incidentally, R OV was calculated by the continuous product of the water recovery of each participating treatment unit.

Besides, the long-term operation stabilities and anti-fouling properties of both tailored TFN-CQDs and commercial BW30 membranes were assessed. Dynamic HDE permeation tests were conducted for time duration 72 h at their respective optimal R NF/R RO conditions. More precisely, based on the measured data including pure water flux after the initial 8 h (J w1) and permeate flux after filtrating HDE for additional 56 h (J p), as well as recovered flux after the final 8 h of physical flushing (J w2), fouling resistance indexes comprising the flux recovery ratio (FRR), reversible fouling ratio (R r), irreversible fouling ratio (R ir) of the membranes could be successfully calculated [23], which were calculated from Eqs. (4)–(6).
(4)
F R R
( % )
=
J
w 2

× 100 %
(5)
R r

i r

w 1
× 100 %

Chemical constitution, size and surface morphology of the synthesized CQDs precursor were analyzed by FTIR spectroscopy (NEXUS, America), TEM (JEM-2100, Japan) and AFM (MultiMode 8, America), respectively. Nominal pore size, pore size distribution (PSD) and surface charge of the fabricated NF membranes were detected by neutral polyethylene glycol rejection tests [24] and the self-made electrokinetic measurement device [23]. The changes of surface topographies, wettabilities and chemical compositions for the adopted membranes during HDE advanced reclamation processes were determined by FESEM (JMS-6390LV, Japan), CA goniometer (Theta Attension, Sweden) and ATR-FTIR spectroscopy (Spectrum 400 F, America), respectively. Based on the fact that keratin proteins released from animal hairs could be stained with fluorescein isothiocyanate dye, the thickness and layout of membrane fouling layer were observed by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, TCS SP8 MP, Germany) at excitation of 490 nm and emission of 530 nm, and the depth was 20 μm with 1.5 μm increasement.

The feedwater and produced water of each treatment unit were collected and used for determination of pH, chromaticity, NH4–N and COD, respectively. Chromaticity and NH3–N were determined by dilution multiple method (GB 11903–89) and Nessler's reagent colorimetric method, respectively. Water quality indexes including total dissolved solids (TDS), turbidity and dissolved organic carbon (DOC) of the related HDE samples were measured by conductivity meter (DDS-307 A, China), turbidimeter (HI83414, Italy) and TOC/TNb analyzer (Vario, Germany), respectively. Concentrations of Mg2+ and Ca2+ ions were analyzed using ion chromatography (ICS-3000, USA) to obtain the corresponding total hardness. Average particle sizes of HDE and flocculation supernatants were detected by laser scattering particle analyzer (S3500, Microtrac, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics and flocculation performance of CLF-10
ce:section.ce:para: Physicochemical properties as well as flocculating performance of CLF-10 were depicted in Fig. 2 . As shown in Fig. 2a, the emergence of three peaks (from left to right) in FTIR spectrum ascribed to the stretching of N–N bond, C–N bond and CN bond, respectively, as well as the detection of N 1s signal from XPS wide scan spectrum verified that the quaternary ammonium group was successfully grafted onto CLF-10. A considerable positive charge of 16.8 mV and satisfactory hydrophilicity with contact angle (CA) of 42.5° possessed by CLF-10 illustrated in Fig. 2b made it easier to agglomerate destabilized colloidal particles in HDE and further promote the removal rates of contaminants. The corresponding AFM scanning and height distribution results could be seen in Fig. 2(c–d). In general, CLF-10 presented a clear trunk with fluffy branching structure, and had a narrow and neat height distribution mainly between 120 nm and 190 nm. This allows CLF-10 to efficiently cluster the vast majority of colloidal contaminants due to its more active sites and smaller steric hindrance effect. Hence, its removal rates of turbidity, chromaticity and DOC for heavily contaminated HDE primary pretreatment reached almost 100%, 95.3%% and 71.9%, respectively (see Table 1). Fig. 2e also demonstrated that the mean particle diameter of HDE supernatant after pretreated by CLF-10 increased from 32.8 nm to 98.2 nm, and specifically, the doping of CLF-10 successfully polarized the original wide plateau-shaped particle size distribution, thus helping the subsequent secondary pretreatment (UF unit) to effectively reject most organic pollutants in the supernatant. Meanwhile, the optical image of flocs under the action of CLF-10 was exhibited in Fig. 2f. Apparently, the large average flocs area of 0.27 mm2 was formed by CLF-10, again validated its excellent bridging interaction among colloids and macro/medium molecular organics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical properties of the resultant TFC/TFN membranes skin layers

ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 a features the ATR-FTIR spectra of PES substrate, TFC-0, TFC-MTH and TFN-CQDs membranes. For PES supporting layer, the absorption peaks at 1317 cm−1 and 1240 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of C–O–C and C–SO2–C bonds. After both peaks vanished in the spectra of as-prepared TFC/TFN membranes, two new weak peaks at 1610 cm−1 and 1560 cm−1 appeared, which could be separately attributed to the CO stretching and N–H bending vibrations in amide groups, identifying the successful formation of PA layer through interfacial polymerization. Also, the peak at 3380 cm−1 in the spectrum of TFN-CQDs membrane represented –OH stretching vibration, which might arise from the incorporation of CQDs. Additionally, after the modification, the intensities of both peaks associated with the amide group described above decreased gradually and followed an order: TFC-0 > TFC-MTH > TFN-CQDs, indicating the crosslinking degree of PA dense layer decreased continuously during this process. This is probably due to the larger molecular size of MTH as an alternative amine monomer and the subsequent dragging effect derived from intermolecular hydrogen bonding between them after doping CQDs, both of which decreased the diffusion rates of amine monomers to the oil phase interface. Actually, wide-scan XPS analysis presented in Fig. 3b also confirmed this speculation, as evidenced by the gradually decreasing N/O ratio [25] from 0.62 for TFC-0 membrane to 0.40 for TFN-CQDs membrane.

Morphologies of the as-prepared TFC/TFN membranes skin layers incorporated with MTH/CQDs were characterized by FESEM and AFM, respectively, as depicted in Fig. 4 . A dense polyamide layer with ridge-and-valley structure was visualized on the surface of pristine TFC-0 membrane, deriving from the fast reaction rate of the interfacial polymerization between MPD and TMC. Meantime, after partial replacing MPD with MTH monomer, TFC-MTH membrane exhibited relatively conspicuous nodules structures with smoother surface, which is attributed to the decrease in cross-linking as described earlier. It was difficult for the employed hydrochlorinated MTH to penetrate into the organic phase due to its more hydrophilic property, which slowed down the growth rate of ridges and minimized surface roughness. Subsequently, after CQDs doping modification, membrane surface roughness was further significantly reduced, which might be due to the fact that in addition to the reduced cross-linking degree, it also benefits from the small size and well dispersion in the polymer matrix of zero-dimensional CQDs itself. These changing trends were qualitatively consistent with the three-dimensional AFM results (see Fig. 4(g–i)), in which case, the root mean square roughness (RMS) values of TFC-0, TFC-MTH and TFN-CQDs membranes successively decreased from 98.4 nm to 63.7 nm and 38.3 nm. Cross-sectional morphologies of these resultant membranes depicted in Fig. 4(d–f) manifested that their skin-layer thickness gradually declined from 287 nm to 228 nm, which would undoubtedly be regarded as a further optimization of membrane microstructure to increase membrane permeability for the next advanced reclamation of HDE. Similar to the decreasing roughness data, this phenomenon may also be caused by doping nanoparticles impeded the diffusion of MPD/MTH monomer and further retarded the formation of PA active layer [26,27].

Water CAs and zeta potentials tested using 1.0 mmol L−1 KCl aqueous solution of the resultant TFN membranes functional layers are presented in Fig. 5 . It could be observed from Fig. 5a that after partial alternative doping of MTH and continuous incorporating of CQDs, the measured static CA at 20 s markedly decreased from 95.4° for neat TFC-0 membrane to 72.5° and 62.7° for TFC-MTH and TFN-CQDs membranes, respectively. As mentioned earlier, for one thing, MTH dissolved in the aqueous phase in the form of polar hydrocarbonate and participated in the subsequent polymerization reaction. For another, as an alternative amine reactive monomer, due to the large steric hindrance effect of MTH itself (see Fig. 1), the polymerization reaction activity and the degree of cross-linking of PA layer are reduced, which result in more unreactive acyl chloride groups remaining on the TFC-MTH membrane surface compared with pristine membrane. Both favorable factors contributed to the markedly improvement for the modified membrane hydrophilicity, although the reduced membrane surface roughness (see AFM data in Fig. 4) was not conducive to this change in the meantime. In addition to containing a considerable number of polar oxygen-containing functional groups, due to the interference effect of the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds between CQDs and MPD, the cross-linking degree of PA functional layer continuously decreased. Therefore, the hydrophilicity of TFN-CQDs membrane was further improved. As shown from the typical zeta potential curves (see Fig. 5b) dependence of pH value in the range of 3.0–10.0, compared with TFC-0 membrane, partial alternative doping of MTH and continuous incorporating of CQDs resulted in a slight and noticeable increase, respectively, in the negative charge of the resultant TFC/TFN membranes active layers. This is primarily owing to the associated gradual decrease in the cross-linking degree of PA layer leading to more network terminals and prompting more dominant acyl chloride groups to remain on the membrane surface. Besides, after doping CQDs, the abundant carboxyl and hydroxyl groups at their edges were also introduced into the membrane functional layer, resulting in increased electronegativity of the membrane surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nominal pore size, PSD and porosity
ce:section.ce:para: Pore size distribution and porosity of the resultant TFC-0, TFC-MTH and TFN-CQDs membranes skin-layers are analyzed and depicted in Fig. 6 . Apparently, compared with the counterpart, the PSD probability density function curves of both TFC-MTH and TFN-CQDs membranes moved towards the direction of slightly larger pore size without worsening dispersion degree of PSD, and the statistical nominal pore size followed an order: TFN-CQDs (0.46 nm) > TFC-MTH (0.45 nm) > TFC-0 (0.43 nm). Meanwhile, the porosity of TFC-MTH and TFN-CQDs membranes also increased from 23.0% for TFC-0 membrane to 35.5% and 41.6%, respectively. This successful microstructure regulation in line with expectation is mainly attributed to the following factors. Firstly, doping MTH could generates PA skin-layer with larger pore size or even free volume due to its longer molecular chain and lower density of amine active site. Secondly, the poor diffusivity of MTH and the dragging effect of incorporating CQDs on MPD diffusion are both conducive to the occurrence of lower cross-linking degree, which leads to the moderate increase of membrane pore size. Besides, both additional nanochannels provided by CQDs itself and voids formed at the interfaces between CQDs and PA matrix would also expand the porosity of TFN membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of TFN-CQDs membrane for advanced reclamation of HDE
ce:section.ce:para: As previously discussed, it was demonstrated that partial alternative doping of MTH and continuous incorporating of CQDs could result in the controllable adjustment to the as-prepared NF membranes with functional layers composed of desired both skin features (smoother, more hydrophilic and negatively charged) and loose body (enlargement in pore size and porosity). Such modifications in the resultant membrane characteristics will be directly beneficial to their perm-selectivities and antifouling properties for advanced reclamation of HDE. To verify and evaluate this possible performance improvement, the optimal TFN-CQDs membrane was selected and used in comparative studies with commercial BW30 membrane. As illustrated in Fig. 7 , separation performance between both membranes showed a significant difference in the process of ever-increasing permeate recovery rate when using the same influent after the optimized two-stage pretreatment. Firstly, the average flux of TFN-CQDs membrane was approximately 4.5 times higher than that of BW30 membrane. Secondly, the former showed a slighter flux decline trend. For instance, at the end of the running test, the permeate fluxes of TFN and BW membranes dropped by 20.2% and 22.9%, respectively, even though their influent concentration factors were expanded to about 25 and 10 accordingly. Thirdly, although DOC and TDS rejection rates for TFN-CQDs membrane were slightly and controllably lower than those for the latter, it could be surprisingly found that both indexes in the permeate still remained at a low level (TDS < 250 mg L−1 and DOC < 2.0 mg L−1) until R NF increased to 96.0% at the end of the experiment. Of course, considering that once R NF exceeded 93.1%, the rejection rate of DOC decreased sharply due to the non-negligible concentration factor and membrane fouling effect, therefore, 93.1% was identified as the optimal R NF, which successfully guarantees the final product water quality (1.13 mg L−1 of DOC, 91.2 mg L−1 of TDS and 20.7 mg CaCO3·L−1 of total hardness (see Table 1)). As shown in Table 1, other indexes such as COD, NH4–N, and Si4+ in TFN-CQDs permeate were also slightly higher than those of its counterpart at their respective optimal permeate recovery rates. Of course, these controlled declines in series of contaminants removal efficiencies of the modified membrane are compensated by increasing permeability by several folds and reducing brine stream substantially. At this moment, TFN-CQDs permeate was far better than the most harsh discharge standards for dyeing industry in GB4287-92 and feasible to replace the existing fresh water during hairwork dyeing process [28]. Accordingly, the optimal R OV of HDE in flocculation-UF-NF hybrid system was confirmed as 91.2% based on its definition.

Meanwhile, under their respective optimum R NF/R RO conditions, the antifouling properties of TFN-CQDs and BW30 membranes were studied in a long-term operation lasted for another 72 h, respectively. The corresponding fouling resistance results are also appended in Fig. 7a. Obviously, the incorporation of CQDs did enhance the antifouling performance of TFN-CQDs membrane against HDE solution, which might be attributed to the improved surface hydrophilicity, electronegativity and decreased surface roughness. In fact, after physical flushing, TFN-CQDs membrane has shown higher flux recovery ratio and lower irreversible and reversible fouling ratios. More specifically, FRR increased from 91.8% for BW30 membrane to 93.2% for TFN-CQDs membrane, while R r and R ir decreased from 19.5% to 8.2% for the former to 7.0% and 6.8% for the latter. In addition, this was highly consistent with the observation in Fig. 7 that within the same recovery increment range, both permeate flux and TDS/DOC rejection of TFN-CQDs membrane exhibited relatively slow attenuation trends.

Water CAs, ATR-FTIR, CLSM scanning results (depicted in Fig. 8 ) of the virgin/fouled TFN-CQDs membrane and its counterpart were used to detect their changes in surface wettability, chemical constitution and fouling layer thickness during advanced HDE reclamation process. As shown in Fig. 8a, under the same initial influent quality and two-stage pretreatment conditions, CAs of both fouled BW30 and TFN-CQDs membranes increased slightly (by 1.6° and 0.9°), which might be attributed to the unavoidable deposition of the residual small-sized hydrophobic organics with the aid of survived CLFs fragments onto membrane surface. But the increment in BW30 membrane was nearly twice as high as that of TFN-CQDs membrane, which verified again the excellent fouling resistance possessed by TFN-CQDs membrane derived from its antiadhesion surface and thin skin-layer thickness, even though suffering from severe fouling potential at higher final permeate recovery rate. Fig. 8(b–c) describe the variations of chemical structures of BW30 and TFN-CQDs membranes after HDE reuse. Virgin BW30 and TFN-CQDs membranes exhibited characteristic absorbance peaks at 1560 cm−1 (N–H bending vibration), 1663 cm−1 (CO stretching in amide groups). The intensities of these two peaks enhanced and weakened significantly as well as a new peak appeared at 1720 cm−1 (CO bond in carboxylic acid groups derived from dyes and surfactants) after permeation. Interestingly, the peak strength variations for fouled BW30 membrane were obviously greater than those of fouled TFN-CQDs membrane, which also proved that the foulant deposition quantity onto the latter surface was less. Fig. 8(d–f) illustrate the thickness and fluorescence intensity of the layer of contaminants deposited on the surface of fouled BW30/TFN-CQDs membrane. By contrasting the images/data, it could be seen clearly that TFN-CQDs membrane surface showed less protein coverage and weaker fluorescence intensity. More precisely, the fouling layer thickness of fouled TFN-CQDs membrane was 2.0 μm thinner than that of BW30 membrane. All these forceful microscopic phenomena suggested that under the same recycling conditions, the modified TFN-CQDs membrane had shown satisfyingly improved fouling resistance than its counterpart in the process of achieving the highest possible permeate recovery, which are consistent with the fouling tendency indicated by the attenuation degree of separation performance (see Fig. 7).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusing performance comparison with other related studies
ce:section.ce:para: To compare the perm-selectivity and antifouling property of the optimal TFN-CQDs membrane with other reported permeate membranes for series of textile effluents recycling, the relevant experimental data were comprehensively collected in Table 2 [2,9,10,29–32]. Apparently, the as-prepared TFN-CQDs membrane exhibited an impressively qualified and stable recycle rate of HDE (93.1%) when taking both suitable long-term separating performance and bearable membrane fouling into consideration. Specifically, it possessed high water permeance of 4.31 L m−2 h−1 bar−1, rejection for small-sized organics (≥97.3%) and salt (≥96.4%) and good antifouling ability (86.2% of FRR) operated for 72 h, which were superior than those of most other permeate membranes. Besides, the economical and feasible pretreatment steps including less dosage (only 100 mg L−1) of biodegradable CLF-10 flocculant and cheap UF module made the optimized reuse process more attractive.

In this study, a series of custom-tailoring tight nanocomposite membranes were prepared for advanced reclamation of HDE. With the aid of predetermined dual-stage pretreatment including less dosage (only 100 mg L−1) of biodegradable CLF-10 flocculant and cheap UF module, the laboratory-scale hybrid system yielded an impressive R OV of 91.2% for standard HDE reuse not sacrificed for membrane anti-fouling performance and stable long-term operation. This is because partial alternative doping of MTH and continuous incorporating of CQDs could effectively improve both skin features (hydrophilicity, smoothness and electronegativity) and nanoporous loose body (porosity, pore size) of the resultant membranes functional layers. While the DOC, TDS and total hardness rejection rates decreased controllably towards the critical thresholds specified in the standard of reusing, in constant compromise between the enhanced electrostatic repulsion effect and the weakened steric hindrance effect. Consequently, the optimal TFN-CQDs membrane showed high water permeance of 4.31 L m−2 h−1 bar−1, rejection for small-sized organics (≥97.3%) and salt (≥96.4%) and good antifouling ability (86.2% of FRR) operated for 72 h, which were superior than those of most other reported permeate membranes. In brief, the modified TFN-CQDs membrane shows great potential in high-efficient and advanced reclamation of HDE against a background of continuously minimizing waste (brine) stream.
Yuefei Song: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing-Reviewing and editing.
Xueshuang Bai: Methodology, Software, Validation, Writing-Original Draft.
Nan Zhang: Investigation, Methodology.
Zhaoyu Lu: Investigation.
Mengyao Zhu: Software, Visualization.
Yongxin Wang: Data curation.
Kai Jiang: Investigation, Writing-Reviewing and editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Henan provincial key science and technology research project (No. 192102210045).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.memsci.2021.119580.
